    16|---
    17|
    18|# Medical Records and Bills Collection
    19|
    20|> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The records collection workflow follows this sequence:
    25|
    26|1. **Provider Identification**: Confirm all treatment providers with the client, including ER, PCP, specialists, imaging
    27|2. **Request Preparation**: Generate HIPAA medical authorization forms and formal records/billing request letters for each provider
    28|3. **Request Delivery**: Send via fax (using RingCentral or similar) and/or email to the provider's records department
    29|4. **Response Tracking**: Track which providers have responded with records vs. bills vs. both
    30|5. **Follow-Up**: If no response within 2-3 weeks, follow up
    31|6. **Client Assistance**: Ask clients to help obtain records from their patient portal (MyChart) when formal requests are delayed
    32|7. **Completeness Check**: Verify all providers are accounted for before moving to demand
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case B, records/billing requests were sent to St. Elizabeth Florence Hospital via email to a specific contact (Dawn.Probst@stelizabeth.com). The billing info was returned same day. However, getting complete records from Christ Hospital Physicians Primary Care took significantly longer.
    37|- In Case C, records were requested from Baptist Health Louisville via fax on 4/29/2024. Records were received by 5/13/2024 (14 days). A task to "review records and add to Timeline" was created once received.
    38|- In Case B, the client's spouse provided medical visit details from the patient portal (MyChart) while waiting for formal records, which helped move the case forward.
    39|- In Case C, the attorney flagged that records collection had stalled with "no action" for a month, emphasizing that negotiation cannot proceed without complete records.
    40|- In Case D (VA patient), records requests to VA Medical Center Lexington took over 12 months to yield results. Initial request mailed Nov 2023; VA OGC responded Jul 2024. Even after receiving records, they were incomplete — the client reported additional MRIs, physical therapy (VA IPRP program), and injections not in the file. The firm had to ask the client to review records and identify what was missing. The attorney noted: "We're missing a lot of stuff, and that's just part and parcel to the VA."
    41|- In Case D (premises liability, litigation), records had to be requested as CERTIFIED copies for use in litigation — standard copies were insufficient. The certification required correction (date range "04/2014 thru Current"). Records were delivered via Box.com file sharing from ROI quality analysts (e.g., Medicopy). Multiple providers were involved: Grace Health (PCP), Norton/Khalily (orthopedics), CHI St. Joseph (orthopedics + radiology), Baptist Health Corbin, and Corbin Imaging. Records were re-requested to get updated records during active litigation.
    42|- In Case E (auto accident), records review during demand preparation revealed referrals to providers not previously on file (White House Clinics, King's Eye Care). These had to be added and their records requested, delaying demand preparation. Records from one provider (H2 Health) were requested by calling, faxing, and re-faxing over weeks.
    43|- In Case D (20+ providers across 4 states), initial records/bills requests were sent in Feb-Apr 2023, but many providers had not responded by mid-2024, requiring a complete second round of "Certified Medical Records & Billing Request" letters in Aug 2024 — nearly 18 months after the first round. Multiple channels were used simultaneously (email, fax via RingCentral, ChartSwap, EpicLink). Provider confusion arose when billing names didn't match clinical names (e.g., "Norton Leatherman" vs "Norton Neuro & Spine" returned "No Records" despite bills existing).
    44|- In Case F (WC case), records were requested from Medical Center of Scottsville and Riverside Orthopedics via fax within the first week of file setup. The Med Center required a DocuSign-verified authorization (the standard fax copy was rejected), adding a round-trip delay. The provider also needed clarification on scope ("only ER records or all records"). Records arrived piecemeal — radiology CD first, then billing statements separately. A third-party billing entity (TeamHealth) sent bills that were associated with the hospital visit but under a different entity name. Multiple new providers were added over 2+ years as treatment progressed (pain management, shoulder injections, MRI facilities).
    45|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), records collection involved an air ambulance service (Air Evac EMS) requiring fax to a distant location. ChartSwap was used for TriStar Skyline Medical Center records retrieval. The case involved records from providers across multiple states (TN hospital where air-lifted, KY local providers). The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, making complete billing documentation critical.
    46|- In Case G (MVA, companion cases), records collection was complicated by a companion case (daughter) requiring separate hospital records from Children's Hospital. The client was asked to obtain companion records but failed to do so over months (Oct 2024 to Feb 2025+). Chiropractic records from Southgate Chiropractic were also needed.
    47|- In Case I (MVA), records were requested from UofL Health St. Mary & Elizabeth Hospital and chiropractic provider via fax within the first week. Provider sent records via ChartSwap platform. The PIP adjuster proactively asked about medical records even before the firm's formal requests were complete.
    48|- In a PL case (retail slip-and-fall, 10+ providers), records collection extended over 2+ years. Even during active litigation (July 2025), the firm was still making outbound calls to providers (orthopedics, therapy, PCP) for outstanding records and bills. The medical authorization form was rejected by one provider because it was missing the date of the patient's signature and their DOB — requiring client callback and resubmission, confirming that incomplete authorization forms are a recurring records collection bottleneck. ChartSwap was used as an intermediary for some provider records.
    49|- In an MVA case with companion plaintiffs, certified medical records were requested from a university hospital and chiropractic provider for litigation. A billing error was discovered in the chiropractic records (incorrect certified billing amount) requiring correction correspondence. This confirms that certified records must be audited — providers can certify incorrect billing amounts. Bills from EMS providers were also collected for PIP submission. The case used EvenUP for case valuation, requiring complete records before the evaluation could be generated.
    50|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, DOI 8/2023), records were requested from 6 providers including EMS, a university hospital system (separate hospital and physician entities), chiropractic, pharmacy, and pain management. The hospital system had distinct billing entities — hospital bills were requested separately from physician bills, and each was received on different dates (spanning Nov-Dec 2024). The PIP claim was set up same day and PIP info provided to the hospital immediately. The firm also requested and received the police report from the out-of-state police department (New Albany, IN) — the report was not immediately available the day after the accident and required follow-up.
    51|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), records collection was complicated by provider misidentification. The firm initially requested records from "CareTenders" (a home health agency), only to discover no records existed — the actual provider was a different home health company. The surviving spouse later provided a calendar from the correct provider, enabling the correct request. Records from one diagnostic imaging provider were requested via ChartSwap, while records from the hospital required a separate LOR to the legal department before records could be released. Bills for the home health provider remained outstanding even 3+ years later during active litigation — the attorney noted during deposition prep that bills were missing. This confirms that provider misidentification is a persistent risk, especially for home health and similar ancillary providers where patients may not know the exact entity name.
    52|- In a treatment-phase case (DOI 1/2026), the firm sent records/bills requests via fax to PIP carrier and directly to the provider (Starlite Chiropractic) within weeks of file setup. The task "Confirm all medical providers to date including PCP" was created ~7 weeks post-DOI, confirming the periodic provider verification workflow. A separate request was faxed to the provider asking to "Please forward the records/bills" — demonstrating the direct provider request pattern alongside PIP submission.
    53|- In a companion case (DOI 11/2025), records requests were sent to UofL Hospital and chiropractic providers for multiple family members. The PIP adjuster's detailed billing analysis (denying specific CPT codes per patient) revealed that the PIP carrier independently reviews medical records and determines which services were "not rendered or were labeled incorrectly" — adding a new dimension to records collection where the PIP carrier's records review may conflict with the firm's records.
    54|- In a file-setup case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "unable to locate PR" (police report) and followed up with a faxed crash report request. The report was eventually received via fax. This is consistent with the pattern of police report unavailability early in the case, requiring multiple request methods (online search, then fax request).
    55|- In 9 additional small cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-2/2026), records collection patterns confirmed and expanded: (1) In a medical malpractice case, records from a children's hospital system (3 departments: medical group, urology, women's and children's) were all received on the same day, approximately 5 weeks after intake — hospital systems with centralized records departments respond faster than private practices; (2) In a case with a single orthopedic provider, records/bills requests were sent via fax to the provider's main number, with a follow-up fax sent 2 weeks later — confirming the standard 14-day follow-up cadence; (3) In a file-setup-phase case with the client still actively treating at a chiropractic provider, no records request was sent — records requests are deferred until treatment completion; (4) Urgent care clinic records were obtained through direct email correspondence with the clinic's front desk staff (not a medical records department), with records attached to the email response — smaller clinics have less formal records processes; (5) One urgent care clinic provided records and billing in response to a PIP exhaustion inquiry, demonstrating that billing-related correspondence can serve as a records collection trigger; (6) In companion cases sharing a chiropractic provider, records/bills for both companions were requested and received independently, with one companion's records arriving the same day as the request (same-day turnaround) while the other companion's took several weeks.
    56|- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), records collection patterns confirmed existing workflow: (1) records/billing requests sent via fax and email within the first 1-2 weeks of file setup; (2) requests directed to university hospital systems (UofL Health), community hospitals, EMS providers, and chiropractic offices; (3) UofL Health records returned via ROI department with confirmation ("Received" from ROI tech); (4) hospital records and bills sometimes received on separate dates (e.g., records on day 9, bills on day 10 from same provider); (5) EMS records required separate fax request to EMS provider (Louisville Metro EMS, Anchorage EMS); (6) one case had only a single minimal-treatment provider (walk-in clinic, $99 bill) — records and bills were received together within 9 days; (7) in a slip-and-fall case managed by a third-party claims administrator, records from a chiropractic provider revealed that treatment was related to a prior MVA and not the slip-and-fall incident — creating a treatment-relatedness dispute; (8) PIP carriers (State Farm, Shelter Insurance) were sent records/billing requests in parallel with provider requests, with the standard language "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (9) in 3 companion cases, records were collected independently for each client even when they shared the same accident and some of the same providers.
    57|
    58|## Agent Guidance
    59|
    60|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    61|", "total_lines": 86, "file_size": 15273, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=61 to continue reading (showing 1-60 of 86 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    56|- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), records collection patterns confirmed existing workflow: (1) records/billing requests sent via fax and email within the first 1-2 weeks of file setup; (2) requests directed to university hospital systems (UofL Health), community hospitals, EMS providers, and chiropractic offices; (3) UofL Health records returned via ROI department with confirmation ("Received" from ROI tech); (4) hospital records and bills sometimes received on separate dates (e.g., records on day 9, bills on day 10 from same provider); (5) EMS records required separate fax request to EMS provider (Louisville Metro EMS, Anchorage EMS); (6) one case had only a single minimal-treatment provider (walk-in clinic, $99 bill) — records and bills were received together within 9 days; (7) in a slip-and-fall case managed by a third-party claims administrator, records from a chiropractic provider revealed that treatment was related to a prior MVA and not the slip-and-fall incident — creating a treatment-relatedness dispute; (8) PIP carriers (State Farm, Shelter Insurance) were sent records/billing requests in parallel with provider requests, with the standard language "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (9) in 3 companion cases, records were collected independently for each client even when they shared the same accident and some of the same providers.
    57|
    58|## Agent Guidance
    59|
    60|1. Create a provider checklist during file setup with records requested/received dates for each
    61|2. Send HIPAA authorizations and record requests within the first week of file setup
    62|3. Use both fax and email when possible — some providers respond faster to one channel
    63|4. Set follow-up tasks at 14-day intervals for each outstanding request
    64|5. Encourage clients to share records from patient portals as an interim measure
    65|6. Track records and bills separately — receiving one does not mean you have the other
    66|7. Do not move to demand preparation until ALL providers have been accounted for
    67|8. When the attorney asks about case status, have the records collection status ready
    68|
    69|## Pitfalls
    70|
    71|- Not confirming all providers with the client early (discovering a provider late delays everything)
    72|- Sending requests and not tracking responses
    73|- Not distinguishing between records received and bills received (you need both)
    74|- Letting the client's medical portal records substitute for formal provider records in the demand
    75|- Waiting too long to follow up on non-responsive providers
    76|- Requesting records under a billing entity name that differs from the clinical entity name (causes "no records found" responses)
    77|- Not anticipating that multi-state providers require different request methods and response timelines
    78|
    79|## Related
    80|
    81|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP needs bills to process; records collection feeds PIP workflow
    82|- [[demand-timing-strategy]] — demand cannot be sent until records are complete
    83|- [[provider-communication-patterns]] — how different providers respond to requests
    84|- [[government-entity-records-collection]] — VA and other government providers require distinct handling with much longer timelines
    85|- [[premises-liability-case-patterns]] — PL cases in litigation require certified records
    86|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — records completion gates the demand phase

messages.content:
{"content": "    30|6. **Check Receipt & Deposit**: Firm receives and deposits check (clearing time applies)
    31|7. **Lien Resolution**: Firm pays outstanding liens (health insurance, PIP subrogation, Anthem/Carelon, Medicaid, etc.)
    32|8. **Fee Calculation**: Attorney fee (typically 33.3% pre-litigation, 40% post-litigation) deducted
    33|9. **Client Disbursement**: Net amount distributed to client via check or wire
    34|10. **Case Closing**: File moved to Closed phase
    35|
    36|### Common Bottlenecks
    37|- **Client fails to sign release**: VineSign resends are common; some clients need multiple reminders
    38|- **Release terms disputed**: PIP subrogation indemnity language or hold harmless clauses may require negotiation between the firm and defense counsel
    39|- **Check not received**: Carriers may delay check issuance; firm must follow up
    40|- **Lien not finalized**: Health insurance lien amounts may change after settlement, requiring updated ledgers
    41|- **Client urgency**: Clients want their money immediately but liens must be resolved first
    42|
    43|### Release Terms Observed
    44|- "Exclusive of PIP/med pay" — settlement is new money, PIP is separate
    45|- "Inclusive of all liens" — settlement amount must cover lien payments
    46|- "Hold harmless and indemnity" — client indemnifies carrier against future lien claims
    47|- "Agreed Order of Dismissal" — for litigated cases, requires court filing
    48|- "Confidentiality" — some releases include non-disclosure terms
    49|
    50|## Evidence
    51|
    52|- In a settled MVA case (DOI 6/2023, KY), the release was sent to the client via VineSign. The client did not see it. Staff asked Justin to resend. Client was told to check email. This pattern of VineSign non-receipt/non-awareness was repeated in multiple cases.
    53|- In an MVA case (DOI 6/2023, KY), the settlement check (#7590) for over $22K was issued. The firm called the client to notify her; she requested the check be mailed rather than picked up. A tracking number was sent via email. This shows the client preference for check delivery varies and should be asked.
    54|- In a settled MVA case (DOI 4/2024, KY), faxed signed and notarized releases (3 releases for 3 plaintiffs) were sent to the adjuster. The adjuster confirmed receipt. However, the client called via translator saying she had 3 days to pay medical bills before collections and needed her settlement check. The firm explained the insurance check had not yet been received. This demonstrates the gap between "settled" and "check received" causes acute client distress.
    55|- In a case (DOI 7/2024, KY), the BI carrier (Travelers) settled the claim but the firm had not returned the signed release months later. The adjuster called repeatedly (5+ voicemails) requesting the release because the PIP carrier was requesting reimbursement and could not be paid until the release was signed. This shows release delays create cascading problems for carriers too.
    56|- In a multi-plaintiff case (DOI 10/2021, KY), defense counsel sent settlement checks but expressly asked "they not be negotiated until your clients signed the releases." The checks were cashed/deposited without releases being returned, prompting defense counsel to demand compliance. This is a serious compliance issue — settlement checks should not be deposited until releases are executed.
    57|- In a case (DOI 3/2023, KY), the settlement at mediation required releases from multiple parties. The release negotiation itself took months — defense counsel and the firm exchanged emails about PIP subrogation indemnity language that was present in one release but not a companion's release. The firm pushed back: "our pre-mediation demands clearly stated that any settlement would be exclusive of PIP or indemnity obligations."
    58|- In 5+ cases, the firm used ZipLiens to process health insurance lien resolution. After settlement, the firm contacted the lien holder (Anthem/Carelon, Medicaid via Conduent, Optum) to obtain final lien amounts and negotiate reductions before disbursement.
    59|- In a case (DOI 5/2022, KY), the BI settled for $7K and the firm then had to determine if a UIM claim was open/pending before fully closing the file, demonstrating that settlement of one claim track does not necessarily close the case.
    60|
    61|## Agent Guidance
    62|
    63|1. When settlement is agreed, immediately send the release to the client via VineSign
    64|2. Follow up within 48 hours if the client has not signed — call and explain the document
    65|3. Once the release is signed, return it to the carrier/defense counsel the same day
    66|4. Track the settlement check issuance — set a 14-day follow-up for check receipt
    67|5. Before disbursement, confirm ALL liens are resolved: health insurance, PIP subrogation, Medicaid, child support, pre-settlement funding
    68|6. Ask the client their preference for receiving the check (mail vs. pickup)
    69|7. Do NOT deposit settlement checks until all releases are fully executed and returned
    70|", "total_lines": 87, "file_size": 7542, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=70 to continue reading (showing 30-69 of 87 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

messages.content:
{"content": "    30|6. **Lien Coordination**: KAC payments interact with health insurance liens (e.g., Medicaid/Conduent) — the firm must track what KAC pays versus what the health insurer paid
    31|7. **Coverage Follow-Up**: The KAC carrier will periodically follow up on coverage status — if BI coverage is eventually accepted, the KAC carrier may seek reimbursement
    32|
    33|### Key Differences from Standard PIP
    34|- KAC requires the client to have no available PIP coverage of their own OR the at-fault driver to have no coverage
    35|- The assigned carrier performs its own investigation into coverage status
    36|- Bill submission may require proof that the treatment is accident-related (similar to PIP relatedness disputes)
    37|- The attorney may negotiate which bills are submitted to KAC (e.g., agreeing during an EUO that certain bills will not be sent to KAC)
    38|- KAC coverage may have different limits than standard PIP
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle with coverage denial), a KAC claim was set up through Nationwide Mutual Insurance Company (claim #617417-GM, later #202111021). The at-fault driver's BI carrier (Allstate) denied coverage due to the stolen vehicle claim, triggering the KAC process.
    43|  - The KAC adjuster (Beth Freeman at Nationwide) managed the claim, coordinating with the firm on bill submissions
    44|  - The adjuster called requesting specific documentation: "Requested copy of Conduent Lien, MRs & MBs"
    45|  - The firm "Reviewed & Sorted currently MRs & MB - Replied & provided requested information"
    46|  - During a separate EUO related to the litigation, the attorney agreed that certain bills (from "Starlite Billing") would NOT be submitted to KAC for payment — demonstrating that the KAC process involves negotiation about covered bills
    47|  - The Conduent (Medicaid) lien of $20,669.08 had to be coordinated with KAC payments
    48|  - A "Follow up on coverage" task was set repeatedly (every 1-2 weeks) tracking whether the KAC carrier had confirmed coverage
    49|  - The KAC carrier investigated coverage status in parallel with the BI carrier's coverage investigation
    50|
    51|## Agent Guidance
    52|
    53|1. When BI coverage is denied or the at-fault driver is uninsured, immediately identify whether a KAC claim is needed
    54|2. File the KAC application promptly — do not wait for the BI coverage dispute to resolve
    55|3. Track the KAC claim separately with its own claim number, adjuster, and follow-up schedule
    56|4. Submit medical bills to the KAC carrier with documentation of accident-relatedness
    57|5. Coordinate with the attorney before agreeing to exclude any bills from KAC submission
    58|6. Track the interaction between KAC payments and Medicaid/health insurance liens — who paid what
    59|7. Set 2-week follow-up tasks to confirm coverage status with the KAC carrier
    60|", "total_lines": 84, "file_size": 6283, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=60 to continue reading (showing 30-59 of 84 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

  - KAC claims were typically set up within the first 2 weeks of file setup, immediately after determining that no standard PIP was available
  - In multiple cases, the KAC carrier (State Farm) was also the PIP carrier assigned to handle medical bill payments — the firm tracked these as standard PIP claims once assigned
  - KAC cases frequently involved Conduent/Medicaid liens running in parallel, creating a three-way coordination between KAC payments, Medicaid liens, and eventual BI settlement

{"content": "  1325|- **"Settled inclusive" vs "exclusive of PIP"**: Critical distinction in release terms; most KY settlements are "exclusive of PIP" (new money)
  1326|- **VineSign for releases**: Universal tool for client release signing; non-receipt/non-awareness is the primary bottleneck
  1327|- **PIP adjuster file closure**: Actively occurring — PIP adjusters close files after 4-6 unreturned calls spanning 3-6 months
  1328|- **Release-to-reimbursement dependency**: BI carriers cannot reimburse PIP until release is signed; creates cascading delays
  1329|
  1330|### New Gaps Identified
  1331|- **Settlement-to-disbursement timeline**: No data on how long the disbursement process actually takes (from settlement agreement to client receiving net check). Would require tracking across more cases.
  1332|- **EvenUp vs manual demand outcomes**: Only 1 case with clear EvenUp attribution; need more data to confirm whether AI demands produce systematically better results.
  1333|", "total_lines": 1332, "file_size": 148358, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

## 2026-04-12 — Batch 2/3 Compilation (55 Settlement/Activity Cases)

**Compiler**: Wiki Compiler Agent (subagent - Batch 2 of 3)
**Cases Processed**: 55 (from /tmp/batch2_3_cases.txt)
**Source Data**:
- 55 KY cases: 53 MVA, 2 premises liability, 1 WC/MVA hybrid
- Activity log counts ranged from 20 to 155 logs per case (total ~3,500 logs processed)
- Cases with >50 logs were sampled (first 10 + last 10 chronologically)
- Case stages observed: full lifecycle from intake through settlement disbursement in 39+ cases
- Key carriers: State Farm, KFB, Progressive, Liberty Mutual, Allstate, USAA, Direct Auto, Root, National General, Geico, Clearcover
- 15 companion case pairs identified (Padgett, Henderson, Mullins, Bush/Norris, Osei, and others)
- 14 KAC cases (State Farm most common assigned carrier)

### Articles Created: 1 concept, 2 connections

1. `lost-wages-verification-workflow` (concept) — New process article: salary/wage verification form workflow, employer signature requirement, lost wages as demand component, WC TTD distinction. Evidence from 10 cases.
2. `franchise-liability-complicates-pl-file-setup` (connection) — How franchise location identification (store → corporate → franchise LLC → registered agent → insurance) adds weeks to PL file setup. Evidence from 2 PL cases.
3. `wc-settlement-uses-form-110-not-release` (connection) — WC settlements use Form 110 + DWC attorney fee approval instead of standard BI release. Evidence from 1 WC case with $12K settlement.

### Articles Updated: 33 concept articles + 11 connection articles

**Concept articles updated (evidence_count increments):**
1. `intake-and-onboarding-workflow` — 108->163 (+55 cases)
2. `case-phase-transitions` — 107->156 (+49 cases)
3. `medical-records-collection` — 89->139 (+50 cases; added KRS 422.317 defense, ChartSwap/EpicLink/MRO platform identification, radiology billing separation)
4. `negotiation-after-demand` — 84->128 (+44 cases)
5. `pip-claims-management` — 79->123 (+44 cases across 10+ PIP carriers)
6. `statute-of-limitations-monitoring` — 70->94 (+24 cases; Virginia 2-year SOL identified)
7. `demand-preparation-workflow` — 50->87 (+37 demand cases)
8. `lien-management-health-insurance` — 27->83 (+56 cases with Conduent, Wellcare, Aetna, Anthem, Rawlings liens)
9. `settlement-disbursement-workflow` — 15->54 (+39 cases; WC Form 110 disbursement variant, defense counsel AOD filing post-disbursement)
10. `client-expectations-management` — 32->42 (+10 cases)
11. `companion-case-management` — 24->39 (+15 companion pairs)
12. `attorney-review-triggers` — 28->38 (+10 cases)
13. `client-frustration-escalation` — 31->34 (+3 cases)
14. `client-communication-through-family` — 23->31 (+8 cases)
15. `adjuster-unreturned-calls-pattern` — 19->29 (+10 cases)
16. `multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases` — 20->28 (+8 cases)
17. `unreachable-client-patterns` — 23->26 (+3 cases; SSN unavailability blocking records)
18. `kentucky-assigned-claims-process` — 12->26 (+14 KAC cases; State Farm as dominant assigned carrier)
19. `case-manager-handoff-patterns` — 15->23 (+8 cases)
20. `medicaid-lien-management` — 11->21 (+10 Conduent cases)
21. `medical-bill-collections-anxiety` — 15->18 (+3 cases)
22. `treatment-gap-defense` — 12->17 (+5 cases)
23. `uim-claim-management` — 14->17 (+3 cases)
24. `adjuster-reassignment-patterns` — 9->14 (+5 cases)
25. `multi-carrier-negotiation` — 9->14 (+5 cases)
26. `medicare-lien-management` — 3->14 (+11 cases; major upgrade)
27. `premises-liability-case-patterns` — 10->12 (+2 PL cases)
28. `police-records-open-records-requests` — 7->12 (+5 cases; BuyCrash usage confirmed)
29. `pre-settlement-funding-obligations` — 7->9 (+2 cases)
30. `case-decline-and-closing-workflow` — 6->9 (+3 cases)
31. `workers-compensation-claim-patterns` — 7->8 (+1 WC/MVA case)
32. `coots-letter-and-uim-trigger` — 4->6 (+2 cases)
33. `workers-compensation-case-patterns` — 4->5 (+1 WC case with Form 110)

**Connection articles updated (evidence_count increments):**
1. `records-completion-gates-demand` — 53->83 (+30 cases)
2. `client-frustration-drives-premature-settlement` — 24->27 (+3 cases)
3. `lien-resolution-delays-disbursement` — 8->18 (+10 cases)
4. `litigation-as-negotiation-leverage` — 14->17 (+3 cases)
5. `pip-delays-cause-collections-anxiety` — 11->16 (+5 cases)
6. `split-settlement-complicates-multi-carrier-math` — 10->15 (+5 cases)
7. `release-delays-block-pip-reimbursement` — 5->13 (+8 cases)
8. `pip-exhaustion-triggers-provider-billing-pressure` — 7->12 (+5 cases)
9. `disputed-liability-forces-litigation` — 8->10 (+2 cases)
10. `coverage-gaps-drive-case-decline` — 6->9 (+3 cases)
11. `sol-emergency-skips-normal-workflow` — 3->4 (+1 case)

### Confidence Upgrades (5 articles)
- `medicare-lien-management`: medium -> high (14 cases)
- `adjuster-reassignment-patterns`: medium -> high (14 cases)
- `police-records-open-records-requests`: medium -> high (12 cases)
- `medicaid-lien-management`: already high (21 cases)
- `multi-carrier-negotiation`: medium -> high (14 cases)

### Key New Patterns Identified

1. **KRS 422.317 records cost defense**: Kentucky statute provides that the first copy of medical records is free. The firm successfully used this defense to close a ScanSTAT invoice for records. This should be standard practice for all KY records vendor invoices.
2. **Lost wages verification is a separate collection track**: Requires client action (employer signature) unlike medical records (provider action). Creates a distinct bottleneck parallel to records collection.
3. **Franchise liability investigation chain**: PL cases at franchise locations require tracing from store manager → corporate → franchise LLC → registered agent → insurance carrier. This added 5+ weeks to file setup in one case.
4. **WC Form 110 settlement mechanism**: WC settlements use Form 110 instead of standard BI release, require DWC attorney fee approval (20% vs 33%), and follow a post-settlement administrative phase absent from PI cases.
5. **State Farm dominates KAC assignments**: In 14 KAC cases, State Farm was the most frequently assigned carrier, suggesting the KAC pool has predictable carrier assignment patterns.
6. **Companion cases with shared KAC**: Siblings from the same accident each require separate KAC applications, but may be assigned to the same carrier.
7. **"Ready to disburse?" as internal checkpoint**: The firm uses an internal query pattern — attorney asks staff whether settlement check has been received and liens are resolved before authorizing client contact for disbursement.
8. **Defense counsel AOD filing post-disbursement**: In litigated cases, defense counsel may file Agreed Order of Dismissal and Notice of Settlement weeks after the client has already received their settlement check.
9. **Root canal during MVA treatment creates timeline complications**: Dental treatment (root canals) during an MVA case extends the treatment timeline and may be perceived as a treatment gap by adjusters.
10. **Monthly recurring fax pattern indicates automated system**: In one case, the same fax number (Bountiful, UT) sent monthly faxes for 12+ months — likely an automated collections or monitoring system. These should not be treated as actionable communications.

### Gaps Partially Addressed
- **Settlement disbursement**: Now documented from 54+ total cases (was 15, now 54) — the single biggest gap from prior compilations is now well-covered
- **KAC process**: Now documented from 26 total cases with State Farm assignment pattern
- **Medicare involvement**: Now documented from 14 cases (was 3)
- **Lost wages**: First documentation of the wage verification workflow

### Remaining Gaps
- WC Form 110 settlement outcomes: Only 1 WC settlement observed
- Franchise liability resolution: Only 1 franchise PL case, litigation ongoing
- KRS 422.317 scope: Only 1 documented successful use; need to understand limitations
- Virginia MVA jurisdiction: Only 1 case; SOL apparently passed without filing
- Monthly automated fax identification: No systematic approach to filtering non-actionable faxes"}

+- `police-records-open-records-requests`: medium -> high (12 cases)
+- `medicaid-lien-management`: already high (21 cases)
+- `multi-carrier-negotiation`: medium -> high (14 cases)
+
+### Key New Patterns Identified
+
+1. **KRS 422.317 records cost defense**: Kentucky statute provides that the first copy of medical records is free. The firm successfully used this defense to close a ScanSTAT invoice for records. This should be standard practice for all KY records vendor invoices.
+2. **Lost wages verification is a separate collection track**: Requires client action (employer signature) unlike medical records (provider action). Creates a distinct bottleneck parallel to records collection.
+3. **Franchise liability investigation chain**: PL cases at franchise locations require tracing from store manager → corporate → franchise LLC → registered agent → insurance carrier. This added 5+ weeks to file setup in one case.
+4. **WC Form 110 settlement mechanism**: WC settlements use Form 110 instead of standard BI release, require DWC attorney fee approval (20% vs 33%), and follow a post-settlement administrative phase absent from PI cases.
+5. **State Farm dominates KAC assignments**: In 14 KAC cases, State Farm was the most frequently assigned carrier, suggesting the KAC pool has predictable carrier assignment patterns.
+6. **Companion cases with shared KAC**: Siblings from the same accident each require separate KAC applications, but may be assigned to the same carrier.
+7. **"Ready to disburse?" as internal checkpoint**: The firm uses an internal query pattern — attorney asks staff whether settlement check has been received and liens are resolved before authorizing client contact for disbursement.
+8. **Defense counsel AOD filing post-disbursement**: In litigated cases, defense counsel may file Agreed Order of Dismissal and Notice of Settlement weeks after the client has already received their settlement check.
+9. **Root canal during MVA treatment creates timeline complications**: Dental treatment (root canals) during an MVA case extends the treatment timeline and may be perceived as a treatment gap by adjusters.
+10. **Monthly recurring fax pattern indicates automated system**: In one case, the same fax number (Bountiful, UT) sent monthly faxes for 12+ months — likely an automated collections or monitoring system. These should not be treated as actionable communications.
+
+### Gaps Partially Addressed
+- **Settlement disbursement**: Now documented from 54+ total cases (was 15, now 54) — the single biggest gap from prior compilations is now well-covered
+- **KAC process**: Now documented from 26 total cases with State Farm assignment pattern
+- **Medicare involvement**: Now documented from 14 cases (was 3)
+- **Lost wages**: First documentation of the wage verification workflow
+
+### Remaining Gaps
+- WC Form 110 settlement outcomes: Only 1 WC settlement observed
+- Franchise liability resolution: Only 1 franchise PL case, litigation ongoing
+- KRS 422.317 scope: Only 1 documented successful use; need to understand limitations
+- Virginia MVA jurisdiction: Only 1 case; SOL apparently passed without filing
+- Monthly automated fax identification: No systematic approach to filtering non-actionable faxes
